eval_checks: (a) question/answer correspond: the rendered answer actually answers/fills the question (for cloze, the revealed deletion fits the blank); flag empty answers, answer==question, or clear non-correspondence.

(b) text well-formed: question_text/answer_text non-empty and readable; no unrendered template artifacts ('{{', '}}', 'FrontSide', '{{c1::'), no obviously garbled/broken text; note image-only cards (no usable text).

(c) regex/LaTeX/cloze markup valid & balanced in the raw `fields`: cloze {{cN::...}} well-formed & balanced; MathJax/LaTeX delimiters balanced ( \(..\), \[..\], [$]..[/$], [$$]..[/$$], $$..$$ ); HTML tags reasonably balanced.

cards.question_text: A Mediating variable explains: [...]
cards.answer_text: A Mediating variable explains: Why 2 things are related Example: If someone makes the statement: "Whenever I watch tv at night, I feel tired the next morning" The mediating variable in this case would be that the person didn't get enough sleep because they were watching tv last night. So not getting enough sleep mediates the relationship between tv watching and being tired.

Example: If someone makes the statement: "Whenever I watch tv at night, I feel tired the next morning"

The mediating variable in this case would be that the person didn't get enough sleep because they were watching tv last night. So not getting enough sleep mediates the relationship between tv watching and being tired.

cards.question_text: A Moderating variable affects: [...]
cards.answer_text: A Moderating variable affects: The strength of the relationship between 2 variables For example, if a study is done to see how likely a person will be to cry by watching a series of sad videos on youtube, a moderating variable could be the gender of the person being tested. So perhaps one gender is more likely to cry compared to the other gender so the strength of the relation between crying and watching sad videos will change. Moderating variables can include all sorts of things like location, gender, race, religion, etc.

cards.question_text: A Confounding variable correlates and explains: [...]
cards.answer_text: A Confounding variable correlates and explains: BOTH the IV & DV For example, if someone says: "Whenever I'm driving on nice roads I see clean sidewalks, they will make the assumption that nice roads correlates to clean sidewalks. But perhaps whenever the person is driving on a nice road they are in a nice and rich neighborhood. So the status of the neighborhood is the confounding variable that explains both why the roads are nice and why the sidewalks are clean.
